speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband's parents. To the left is [? Feyga ?] Hochman, her husband, [? Mechel ?] Hochman, my husband's grandmother, Esther Spindell, and she is holding in her hands my husband's older sister, [? Haicha ?] Hochman. They lived in Kozova, and I don't know exactly when that picture was taken. But they lived in Kozova, and they perished in Kozova. They were killed in Kozova. This is a picture-- on top you'll see my husband, Harold Hochman. And to the right, his cousin, Isaac Hochman, Eli Hochman, and Harold Hochman, his cousin. They-- they were together. They were in business together, and they were, to my husband, like a brother. They grew up together, and they took him in. Their father, Simon Hochman, took him in and raised him like a son. And this is before they leave Germany to come to the United States, August 4, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is with Congressman Seymour Halpern, after we brought Esther, when she came to the United States, and he introduced a bill. It was in all the newspapers that a Holocaust survivor found a sister 16 years after the war, and 19 years after the war, she came to be with us. On the top, you will see Congressman Halpern. Next to it is my husband's uncle, Simon Hochman. He's the one who went to Poland to find her, to try to get-- to make her papers Jewish. To the left is myself. In the middle is Esther, and to the right is my husband, Harold. And on the bottom you'll see Linda, Mark, and Florence. This was a joyous, joyous day for us. My husband found his sister. This what we, my sister and I, looked like at her daughter, Eta Blitzer Shaul's wedding. My sister and I married off-- both of us had weddings in 1980. She married off Eta, and I married off Linda. These were the happiest days in our life, and we look like two children. We were so happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is at my niece's, Eta's wedding. We were very, very fortunate then. My grandmother, Esther Stermer, in the middle-- you will see she's sitting there. She was already 90. This was in 1980-- she was about 92, 93 years old. My mother next to her, Henia. You see my sister standing there, and her husband, David Blitzer, and my Aunt Yetta. And Bobby Shaul is standing there, myself-- Sonia-- my uncle, Sam Stermer. And in the background you'll see my uncle, [? Saul ?] Stermer, my Uncle, Nisel Stermer, and my aunt, Peppa Stermer. We were all celebrating. We were very fortunate, because we had our grandmother and our mother were still alive in 1980. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the wedding of my daughter, Linda Hochman Smook. To the left, standing, is my son, Mark Hochman. Next to it is my husband, Harold Hochman. I am there, Sonia Hochman. Next to it is Linda Hochman Smook, Richard Smook, my brother-in-law, David Blitzer, my niece, Eta Blitzer Shaul, her husband, Bobby Shaul. Sitting is my niece, Bonnie Blitzer Stone, now, my mother, Henia my sister, Sima Blitzer, and my daughter, Florence Hochman. It was another wonderful occasion-- it was the happiest day in my life when I married off my first child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my son, Mark Hochman's, wedding to Claudia Bozzi. We are so happy with this marriage. We love them, and it was the happiest day. But unfortunately, my mother and my grandmother was not alive anymore, but the rest of the family came and we celebrated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These are my sister's grandchildren. On top in the hat, wearing a hat, is Henia Eliana Shaul. She lives in Montreal, Canada. Next to her, the big boy, is her older brother, Daniel Shaul. He lives in Montreal. And there, standing behind in the bow, is Juliana Stone, she lives in Boston, Massachusetts. And the little boy, Yehuda Stone, he also lives in Boston. And there's a new addition, there is Jonathan Shaul. That is-- my niece, Eta, and her husband, Bobby, had another baby, a little boy, Jonathan, and they live in Montreal. My sister has a wonderful family, and so do I. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is the book that my grandmother, Esther Stermer, write-- by Esther Stermer. She was a very courageous lady. And because of her ingenuity and her willful life, we all survived. I think she was instrumental in helping us all survive. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Hochman, would you please introduce your husband and your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be delighted to. This is my husband, Harold Hochman. And right here sitting is Andrew Smook. This is my granddaughter, Emily Hochman. This is my daughter, Linda Hochman Smook. My son-in-law, Richard Smook. My wonderful granddaughter, Liza Joy Smook. My daughter, Florence Hochman. My son, Mark Hochman. And my lovely daughter-in-law, Claudia Hochman. We're a wonderful family, and I'm grateful to God that I survived and I could have a family like this. And I hope that my grandchildren will remember this day, and they'll tell their children and their grandchildren of the epic that their grandparents went through during World War II. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to ask, maybe Mark, growing up in a family with two survivors, what do-- what do you think happened to you while you were growing up? How did this affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that it influenced us greatly, because there were many ramifications of such an event that filtered through to the family, the generation after-- primarily the way that they had a lack of a childhood, and that they weren't fortunate enough to-- to have a lifestyle where they were able do the things-- normal things children do and to enjoy their parents when they were growing at a certain age. So I think that lack of experience in their own lives, and then seeing their own children growing and how to interact with them, I think it was very difficult. And I think there were a lot of ramifications for the-- the second generation after the-- that event, the Holocaust. And each of the individuals, I think, bears some form of a scar, in some way or another.

words.text: I think there were a lot of

speaker: interviewer
text: Linda, what would you like to-- no? Florence, would you like to say something to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that they had to fight to survive, so, in a certain way, because of what happened to them, we, in our own way, had to fight to survive what they went through. And it had an effect on our lives, and I hope future generations won't ever have to go through what we we went through in the Holocause. And that's really all I have to say.

words.text: that's really all I have to

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you? Would you like to say something to your grandma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? Really, I'm just really proud of her, that she survived and my grandpa. And I'm happy that they made it to to this day and that-- that they were at my bat mitzvah, and they shared, like, great times with us. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, it's been an experience understanding and knowing what my mother-in-law and father-in-law went through, and it's been a joy having them as my parents. And they've just given a lot to us and their grandchildren and to carry on with the rest of our lives and-- and their grandchildren's lives. It's a-- a history that has to be carried on and appreciated.
